data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Harness strictly beats self-consistency iff the pool contains a non-empty set of questions whose SC winner is wrong but critic recall on those items > 0 while critic precision is high enough that the expected count of correctly-won questions falsely flagged does not exceed the count of wrong-winner questions truly flagged and recoverable.", "mechanism": "Let R = recall on wrong-SC-winner items, P = precision on right-SC-winner items, n_w = count of wrong-SC-winners, n_r = count of right-SC-winners. Harness net gain = R*n_w - [(1-P)*n_r]*(prob_flagged_right_question_flips_to_wrong). Strict advantage requires R*n_w > false-flag harm. False flags only hurt when they flip a correct majority to incorrect; this probability depends on majority margin. So the operative precision is not pool-wide but precision-weighted-by-flip-vulnerability. The mid-zone 1/(k+1) < e < 0.5 is where wrong winners exist and some correct winners have thin margins, making both terms nonzero.", "scope": {"covers": ["mid-zone base error where some SC winners are wrong AND some correct winners have thin margins", "critic recall > 0 on wrong-winner items", "critic precision high enough that false flags on thin-margin correct winners do not outnumber true flags on wrong winners"]}, "excludes": ["e <= 1/(k+1): no wrong SC winners exist, harness cannot gain", "e >= 0.5: SC majority wrong, critic precision becomes critical but SC baseline already broken", "critic recall = 0: no wrong winners recoverable", "critic precision so low that false flips >= true fixes"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "At k=5, e in [0.15, 0.35], critic with precision >= 0.7 and recall >= 0.5: harness accuracy is NOT strictly greater than SC accuracy, measured as harness_acc - sc_acc > 0 on >= 60% of studies", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "At k=5, e in [0.15, 0.35], critic with precision < 0.5 and recall >= 0.5: harness accuracy is strictly greater than SC (refuting the precision-gate prediction)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "False flags on correct-SC-winner items with margin >= 3 votes do not cause answer flips at rate > 10%, confirming that only thin-margin correct winners are vulnerable", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "The predicted threshold precision >= 1 - R*n_w / (n_r * flip_prob) is empirically violated: harness still gains with precision below threshold or fails above it", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "Supersedes 5b9a8d2c753d by grounding the regime boundary in the causal mechanism (flag-and-flip arithmetic) rather than raw precision/recall values. The measured v4-pro hard-set data (recall 1.0, precision 0.125, net 0) is explained: n_w=0 since SC=1.00, so R*n_w=0 regardless of recall; false flags on n_r items existed but did not flip because margins were >= 4 (only 1 error per question). The v4-flash easy-set (precision 0.333, recall 0.25, net 0) is explained: n_w was 1 (base error 0.033, k=5), so at most 1 recoverable item, but recall 0.25 gives expected 0.25 recoveries while low precision generates false flags that offset. Experiment: 500 questions across 4 base-error tiers (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4), k=5 and k=7, 3 critic configurations (high-P/low-R, low-P/high-R, balanced), ~60k tokens per tier-k-critic cell, 12 cells, ~720k tokens total — trimmed to 4 core cells at ~240k tokens."}
